coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic activity of a push-pull phthalocyanine in the presence of reduced and amino functionalized graphene quantum dots towards the electrooxidation of hydrazine
coredata.dc:creator: Centane, Sixolile
Sekhosana, Edward Kutloano
Matshitse, Refilwe
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: We report on the electrochemical behaviour of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) compared to amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (NH2GQDs). Reduction of the GQDs entails the elimination of the excessive carboxyl and hydrogen groups on the GQDs surface, thereby reducing the energy band gap. The energy band gap of graphene is directly proportional to the available oxygen atoms. The two GQD types were conjugated to a novel cobalt phthalocyanine (cobalt tris-(tert-butyl phenoxy)-mono-carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine, CoPc) via covalent and nom-covalent interactions. The resulting conjugates were tested towards the electrooxidation of hydrazine. The conjugates are represented as rGQDs(π)CoPc, NH2(π)CoPc, rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc. The resulting conjugates were adsorbed onto a glassy carbon electrode using the drop and dry method. The lowest limit of detection (LOD) was obtained for rGQDs(π)CoPc.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt phthalocyanines
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrazine
Cyclic voltammetry
Electrocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of cobalt tris-(tert-butylphenoxy) mono carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (CoPc, 3), Scheme 1
Linking of CoPc onto rGQDs and NH2GQDs
Characterization of GQDs and their conjugates with CoPc

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis spectrum of CoPc, Solvent = DMF.
AFM topographic images of NH2GQDs (A) and rGQDs (B).
TEM images of pristine GQDs (a), rGQDs (b) and rGQDs@CoPc (c).
DLS measurements for (a) rGQDs, (b) NH2GQDs, (c) rGQDs@CoPc and (c) NH2GQDs@CoPc.
Raman spectra of (A) rGQDs, NH2GQDs and pristine GQDs; (B) rGQDs@CoPc and rGQDs(π)CoPc.
FT-IR spectra for pristine GQDs, rGQDs and rGQDs@CoPc.

XPS survey scan for rGQDs and NH2GQDs, (B) N 1s high resolution XPS spectra for rGQDs and NH2GQDs, (C) C 1s high resolution spectra for pristine GQDs, rGQDs, NH2GQDs, CoPc, rGQDs@CoPc (b) and NH2GQDs@CoPc.
Cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrodes in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl.
Nyquist plot resulting from bare GCE (a), GCE/rGQDs (b), GCE/CoPc (c), GCE/rGQDs@CoPc (d) and GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc (e) in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl.
Cyclic voltammograms of (A) (a) GCE/CoPc, (b) GCE/rGQDs@CoPc, (c)GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc, (d) GCE/rGQDs and (e) GCE/NH2GQDs, and (B) the indicated modified electrodes. All in 1 mM hydrazine in 0.1 M NaOH.

Stability scans for GCE/CoPc (a) and GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc (b) as examples. Blue = 1st scan and red = 20th scan. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cyclic voltammograms at various scan rates GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc (a), plot of potential vs. log v (b) and plot of current vs. square root scan rate (v1/2) (c), all in 1 mM hydrazine in 0.1 M NaOH. Scan rate = 100 mV/s.

Rotating disk polarization curves for the electrooxidation of 1 Mm hydrazine in 0.1 M NaOH solution as a function of rotation rates (A) and varying concentration of hydrazine (B). Polarization curves for electrooxidation of hydrazine at different surfaces is displayed (C), run at 2500 rpm.
(a) Chronoamperometric scans of GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc at various concentrations, insert = Current vs. hydrazine concentration and (b) plots of Icat/Ibuff vs. t1/2.
Synthetic route to cobalt tris (tert-butyl phenoxy)-monocarboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (CoPc, 3).

(A) Cutting of graphene oxide to form pristine GQDs, (B) the formation of NH2GQDs by reaction of pristine GQDs with ammonia, and (C) formation of rGQDs, (i) the reduction process and (ii) the reduction mechanism.
Covalent (A) and (B) non-covalent (π-π) conjugation complex 3 to rGQDs.
UV–Vis spectra in DMF and DLS sizes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Q bands for MPcs and emission maxima for GQDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are for the corresponding π-stacked conjugates rGQDs(π)CoPc and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS analysis results for GQDs and their conjugates with CoPc.
Electrochemical parameters for hydrazine in 0.1 M NaOH unless otherwise stated.
Limits of detection for hydrazine in comparison with literature values for CoPc derivatives.

row.entry: Co monocarboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine in ππ conjugation with GQDs
Co tetracarboxyphenoxy phthalocyanines in ππ conjugation with GQDs
Co tetraaminophenoxy phthalocyanines in ππ conjugation with GQDs

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic activity of a push-pull phthalocyanine in the presence of reduced and amino functionalized graphene quantum dots towards the electrooxidation of hydrazine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rhodes University, Chemistry, P.O. Box 94, Grahamstown 6139, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the electrochemical behaviour of reduced graphene quantum dots (rGQDs) compared to amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (NH2GQDs). Reduction of the GQDs entails the elimination of the excessive carboxyl and hydrogen groups on the GQDs surface, thereby reducing the energy band gap. The energy band gap of graphene is directly proportional to the available oxygen atoms. The two GQD types were conjugated to a novel cobalt phthalocyanine (cobalt tris-(tert-butyl phenoxy)-mono-carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine, CoPc) via covalent and nom-covalent interactions. The resulting conjugates were tested towards the electrooxidation of hydrazine. The conjugates are represented as rGQDs(π)CoPc, NH2(π)CoPc, rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc. The resulting conjugates were adsorbed onto a glassy carbon electrode using the drop and dry method. The lowest limit of detection (LOD) was obtained for rGQDs(π)CoPc.

ce:section.ce:para: Metallophthalocyanines (MPcs) are known electrocatalysis for a variety of analytes [1]. In electrocatalysis, the MPcs are usually attached onto a conductive electrode. However, the use of MPcs alone as electrode modifiers for electrocatalysis is often hampered by limited conductivity, which affects their electron transfer and electrochemical activity [2]. To overcome this, MPcs have been combined with various nanomaterials such as single walled carbon nanotubes [3] to provide suitable support and enhance conductivity. MPcs have also been employed together with semiconductor quantum dots for electrocatalysis [4–7], with only two examples on MPcs employed with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for electrocatalysis [8,9]. In this work we combine MPcs with GQDs for electrocatalytic detection of hydrazine as a test analyte.

GQDs are sp2 hybridized carbon based nanomaterials with various edge functional moieties which include hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxylic groups [10,11]. The rich structural chemistry provides these GQDs with intrinsic chemical and physical properties. Their π-electron rich structure affords them the ability to interact with other aromatic compounds via π-π interactions. Their edge functional moieties allows for further functionalization or attachment with guest molecules [12,13]. Compared to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs have low toxicity, are highly soluble in various solvents, biocompatible and have a well-defined quantum confinement [14,15]. These properties render GQDs as suitable materials for various applications such as in photovoltaic cells [16], fluorescence sensing and bioimaging [17]. GQDs have recently gained momentum in the field of electrochemical sensing for the detection of glucose oxidase, hydrogen peroxide and heavy metals [18–20]. We have recently reported on the non-covalent (π-π) conjugates of pristine GQDs and MPcs for electrochemical sensing [9]. In this work, we investigate the electrocatalytic activity of a Pc when covalently attachment to GQDs. The electron transfer properties of any material are dependent on its energy band gap. The energy band gap of graphene-based nanomaterials has been reported to be directly proportional to the available oxygen atoms on the surface [21,22]. Reduction of GQDs involves elimination of the excessive functional group moieties thereby reducing the energy band gap and thus enhancing electron transfer properties, hence reduced GQDs are employed in this work.

In this work, we combine amino-functionalized GQDs and reduced GQDs with a cobalt phthalocyanine for electrocatalytic oxidation of hydrazine. The contribution of the GQDs onto the electrocatalytic activity of the MPcs is herein explored. The MPc complex employed in this work is cobalt tris-(tert-butyl phenoxy)-mono-carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (3), containing three tert-butyl substituents acting as electron donating groups (push), and a single COOH as an electron withdrawing group (pull). Such push-pull substituents have been reported to enhance electron delocalization, thus electron transfer properties of complexes [23]. The COOH terminal of the phthalocyanine allowed for covalent linkage to the amino functionalized GQDs. The nanocomposites show improved electrocatalytic properties compared to individual components in some cases. Hydrazine is used as a test analyte since CoPc derivatives have been employed for its electyrocatalysis [24–29].

N, N dimethyl formamide (DMF) and 1,8-diazabicyclo [5.4.0] underc-7-ene (DBU) were purchased from Merck. K4Fe(CN)6, K3Fe(CN)6, graphene oxide (GO), methanol, 1-pentanol, cobalt chloride hexahydrate, dialysis membrane (MW 1.5 kDa), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), (N-hydroxysuccinimide) (NHS), sulphuric acid, potassium permanganate and N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) were purchased at Sigma Aldrich. Millipore water was obtained from Milli-Q Water Systems (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). Stock solution of hydrazine (1 mM) was prepared in 0.1 M sodium hydroxide. 4-(p-tert-Butylphenoxy) (1) and 4-(4-carboxy-phenoxy) (2) phthalonitriles were synthesized as reported in literature [30,31]. All other reagents and solvents were used as received from commercial suppliers.

Cyclic voltammetric and chronoamperometric data were obtained using Autolab potentiostat PGSTAT 302 (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) driven by the General Purpose Electrochemical System data processing software (GPES, software version 4.9). A three-electrode electrochemical cell made of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, platinum wire (Pt) as the counter electrode and silver|silver chloride wire (Ag|AgCl) as a pseudo-reference electrode was used. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies were performed using an Autolab Potentiostat PGSTAT 30 equipped with GPES software version 4.9. The studies were performed between 0.1 Hz and 10 Hz, using a 5 mV rms sinusoidal modulation. A non-linear least squares (NLLS) method based on the EQUIVCRT programme was used for automatic fitting of the obtained EIS data. Rotating disk electrode measurements were performed with a Bioanalytical Systems (BAS) 100B electrochemical workstation with a BAS Rotating Disk Electrode RDE-2. The working electrode was a BAS glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (GC) of 0.28 cm2.
Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM) images were obtained from Zeiss Libra TEM 120 Model operated at 90 kV.

Elemental analysis were done using a Vario-ElementarMicrocube® ELlII while mass spectral data were collected on a Bruker® AutoFLEXIII smart-beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer using α-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid as the matrix in the positive ion mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done using an AXIS Ultra DLD (supplied by Kratos Analytical) using Al (monochromatic) anode equipped with a charge neutralizer, the following parameters were used: the emission was 10 mA, the anode (HT) was 15 kV and the operating pressure below 5 × 10−9 Torr. A hybrid lens was used and resolution to acquire scans was at 160 eV pass energy in slot mode. The centre used for the scans was at 520 eV (width of 1205 eV) with steps at 1 eV and dwell time at 100 ms. The high resolution scans were acquired using 80 eV pass energy in slot mode. A nonlinear least squares curve fitting procedure was used.

Infrared spectra were recorded on a Bruker® Alpha IR (100 FT-IR) spectrophotometer. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nano series, Nano-ZS90. Ground state electronic absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements in tapping mode were carried out with MFP-3D Origin supplied by Asylum research (Oxford instruments company, USA). Drop casting and room temperature drying of the analyte solution on a freshly cleaved mica surface were used for the preparation of samples for AFM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cobalt tris-(tert-butylphenoxy) mono carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (CoPc, 3), Scheme 1
ce:section.ce:para: Complex 3 was synthesized as follows: a mixture of 1-pentanol (3 mL), 4-(p-tert-butylphenoxy) phthalonitrile (1, 150 mg, 0.543 mmol), 4-(4-carboxy-phenoxy) phthalonitrile (2, 28 mg, 0.108 mmol), CoCl2.6H2O (26 mg, 0.108 mmol), and DBU (0.1 mL) was refluxed at 160 °C for 24 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The product CoPc (3) was precipitated out using 1:1 methanol and water. The product was dissolved in a minimum amount of chloroform, and was chromatographed on a Si60 column with chloroform/hexane (9:1) eluent to obtain the desired second fraction. Several chromatographic separations were done on this fraction using 4:1 mixture of ethanol and chloroform to give the desired product, CoPc (3). Yield: 55 mg (43.8%) UV/Vis (DMF): λmax(log ε), 674 (4.96), 613 (4.41). IR (KBr): ν max, cm−1, 3071 (ν OH), 1750 (ν CO) and 2885 (ν CH). Calculated for C69H56CoN8O6: C; 72.05, H; 5.71, N; 9.34, Found: C; 71.75, H; 5.64, N; 9.40. MALDI–TOF MS m/z: Calcd: ([M + H]+) 1152.15. Found: 1153.

GQDs were synthesized following the top-down approach reported by Fan, et al. using GO sheet as the source of carbon, and KMnO4 and H2SO4 as oxidative cutting agents [32] as follows: GO (1 g) was suspended in 60 mL (98%) H2SO4 for 2 h in an ice-water bath. Then, 50 wt% KMnO4 was slowly added with stirring for 0.5 h. The mixture was then heated to 50 °C for another 1 h in order to shear the carbon precursor. The reaction mixture was quenched by pouring ice containing small amounts of hydrogen peroxide, followed by 70 mL millipore water to obtain a yellow transparent solution. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, then the pH was tuned to basic using 1 mol L−1 NaOH solution in an ice bath until black flocculent deposit formed, which was then dialyzed for 72 h using a dialysis membrane (MW 1.5 kDa) to remove excess salts yielding 39.1% GQDs.

The post-fabrication modification of the pristine GQDs to form the amino functionalized NH2GQDs was achieved following a novel approach via EDC-NHS coupling as follows: pristine GQDs were dissolved in water (6 mg/mL) to form a homogenous solution. To activate the carboxylic groups of the GQDs, EDC (0.17 mmol) and NHS (0.13 mmol) were added simultaneously to the GQDs solution and left to stir for 24 h. To perform the amino functionalization, ammonia solution (3 mL) was added. The resulting NH2GQDs were precipitated out using ethanol. The particles were further subjected to dialysis for 24 h to get rid of unreacted materials and salts. The particles were then washed with ethanol using centrifugation at 20,000 rpm for 15 min each cycle.

The rGQDs were synthesized following literature methods with some modification [22,33]. Briefly, to a solution of the pristine GQDs (6 mg/mL) in water, hydrazine hydrate (500 μL) was added. The solution was left to stir for 12 h at room temperature. Ethanol was used to precipitate the nanoparticles and get rid of unreacted material. To achieve absolute purity, the nanoparticles were subject to dialysis for 24 h, and then washed and centrifuged at 20000 rpm for 15 min per cycle. A colour change from charcoal black to a light brown-beige colour was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linking of CoPc onto rGQDs and NH2GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The conjugation is shown in Scheme 3A using rGQDs@CoPc as an example. In two separate vials, solutions of CoPc (3) (0.00867 mmol, 5 mL) in DMF were added. To each vial, DCC (0.004847 mmol) and NHS (0.004847 mmol) were added to activate the carboxylic groups of the CoPc (3), the mixture was left to stir for 24 h. The functionalized GQDs (rGQDs and NH2GQDs, 10 mg each) were then added to each of the vials separately. The mixtures were left to stir for further 24 h, resulting in the conjugates rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc. The resulting conjugates were precipitated and washed by centrifugation with methanol: H2O (1:1) and dried at 70 °C overnight.

GQDs (5 mg/mL) in water were added to a solution of the CoPc (3) in DMF (0.00433 mmol, 2 mL). The resultant solution was subject to sonication for 4 h at room temperature. The reaction was then left to stir for 48 h to form rGQDs(π)CoPc (shown in Scheme 3B as an example) and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc conjugates. The conjugates were washed and centrifuged with methanol: H2O (1:1) and dried at 70 °C overnight to get rid of unreacted material.

The GCE was thoroughly polished to a silver mirror on a Buehler-felt pad, using alumina (<10 μm) and then washed with Millipore water. The electrode was then sonicated for 5 min in Millipore water. In between each polishing step, the electrode was washed with Millipore water to remove impurities. The electrode was modified using the drop and dry method. Modifications were conducted as follows: 5 μL of the (1 mg/mL in DMF) of each of rGQDs@CoPc, NH2GQDs@CoPc, rGQDs(π)CoPc or NH2GQDs(π)CoPc were placed on the GCE followed by drying at 70 °C giving GCE/rGQDs@CoPc, GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc, GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc, respectively. GCE/rGQDs, GCE/NH2GQDs, and GCE/CoPc electrodes were prepared through the same procedure using 5 μL (1 mg/mL in water for the QDs and in DMF for CoPc).

Cobalt tris-(tert-butylphenoxy) monocarboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (3) was formed by the mixed condensation of two different phthalonitriles: 4-(p-tert-butylphenoxy) phthalonitrile (1), 4-(4-carboxy-phenoxy) phthalonitrile (2) in the presence a cobalt salt (Scheme 1). Column chromatography was employed to obtain a pure product from the reaction mixtures. The mass spectra and elemental analysis data obtained for the compound confirmed successful purification as the expected values were in agreement with the experimental values.1H NMR spectra could not be recorded due to the paramagnetic nature of the central Co(II) metal ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and their conjugates with CoPc

ce:section.ce:para: Pristine GQDs were formed by chopping graphene oxide (GO) sheets into pieces by KMnO4/H2SO4, following literature methods [32], to form GQDs containing carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functionalities, Scheme 2A.The amino functionalization of graphene quantum dots to afford NH2GQDs was achieved using EDC coupled with NHS to improve efficiency of the reactions, Scheme 2B.

The reduction of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form reduced graphene quantum (rGQDs) (Scheme 2C(i)) entails the elimination of excessive oxygen containing functional groups on the GQDs surface. The chemical reduction of GQDs has been previously reported using sodium borohydride [35,36]. In this work, GQDs were reduced using hydrazine, following a procedure previously applied for the reduction of graphene based nanomaterials [22,37]. The original structure of GQDs contains mainly hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups on the surface. The resulting structure after chemical reduction is dependent on the reducing agent and the way in which it interacts with the functional groups found in GQDs. The lone pairs on the nitrogen atoms of the hydrazine molecule (N2H4) act as a nucleophile against the electrophilic carbonyl carbon the GQDs, thereby undergoing an electrophilic addition reaction to form a thermally unstable hydrazino alcohol, which undergoes dehydration forming a more stable hydrazone, which result in the elimination of oxygen (Scheme 2C(ii)). The hydroxyl oxygen atoms on the GQDs are removed by hydrazine via electrostatic interactions between the hydroxyl group and the H atoms of the hydrazine via dehydration.

Fig. S1 (Supporting information) shows the UV–Visible absorption and emission spectra for (a) pristine GQDs, (b) NH2GQDs and (c) rGQDs respectively. Broad absorption spectra were observed for all GQDs. The NH2GQDs and rGQDs showed similar photoluminescence (PL) spectra due to bonding and anti-bonding molecular orbitals of both the CC sp2 and amino edge functionalities [38,39]. The blue shift and broadening of PL and absorption of both rGQDs and NH2GQDs (Fig. S1b, c), compared to pristine GQDs (Fig. S1a), Table 1, is attributed to introduction of surface defects onto GQDs upon functionalization (reduction and amino-functionalization), thus providing intermediate steps (n* states) within the π-π* energy gap [35,39].

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) images for the rGQDs and NH2GQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . The topographic heights for the rGQDs and NH2GQDs alone were <1 nm with an average height of ca. 0.3–0.5 nm suggesting mono-layer graphene sheets [40].

Graphene quantum dots and phthalocyanines are structurally flexible compounds, this allows for modification to achieve desired properties for a specific application. The CoPc (3) in this work is a push pull phthalocyanine with a carboxylic group, which allows for covalent conjugation to the amino functionalized graphene quantum dots via an amide bond, Scheme 3A. DCC/NHS coupling was employed to activate the carboxylic group of the CoPc in order to form a covalent bond with NH2 containing GQDs, the conjugates are represented as rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc for rGQDs and NH2GQDs, respectively.

The sp2 carbon framework of the graphene quantum dots and the 18 π-electron system of the Pc allows for π-π interactions between the two compounds (Scheme 3B). The π-stacked GQDs/CoPc complexes were prepared by mixing solutions CoPc and respective GQDs followed by sonification for 4 h at room temperature, to form rGQDs(π)CoPc and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc for rGQDs and NH2GQDs, respectively.

The absorption spectra of the CoPc upon covalent conjugation with rGQDs and NH2GQDs are shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting information). The Q bands for rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc were blue shifted by 6 nm and 4 nm respectively compared to CoPc (3) alone, Table 1. Spectral shifts have also been observed in porphyrins-graphene oxide nanoconjugates [41]. An increase in absorbance was observed for the B bands of CoPc due to absorption by GQDs in the conjugate. Non-covalent interactions of the GQDs to CoPc have no significant effect on the structure of the Pc but an extension to its π-conjugated system. As a result, there was no significant shift in the Q band of the CoPc upon conjugation to GQDs, but an increased B-band absorbance, which may be attributed to the presence of GQDs, Fig. S2b.

The morphology and size distribution of the GQDs and the conjugates were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fig. 3(a)–(c). GQDs alone show aggregation which increases upon conjugation to the CoPc Fig. 3c, making it difficult to determine the sizes, hence DLS was employed for size, Fig. 4 . The rGQDs and NH2GQDs had hydrodynamic size 2.8 nm and 3.2 nm, respectively, this is an increase in size compared to pristine GQDs (2.4 nm) confirming functionalization (Fig. 4), Table 1. An increase in size upon covalent conjugation was observed for both rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc (7.1 nm) and (10.4 nm), respectively (Table 1). Non-covalent conjugation of the respective GQDs to the CoPc showed a similar trend: rGQDs(π)CoPc (8.2 nm) and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc (11.2 nm). The increase in particle size upon conjugation might be due to aggregation as a result of interactions between the MPcs on adjacent NPs via π-π stacking. Pcs are known for their π-π stacking to form H aggregates [34].

Structural changes that occurred on the GQDs during chemical process are reflected in the Raman spectroscopy, Fig. 5 . Graphene quantum dots have two characteristic Raman peaks; G band (sp2) tangential mode and D-disorder band (sp3) breathing mode. The D band located at 1350 cm−1 is assigned to the non-conjugated sp3 carbon defects. The G band at 1590 cm−1 is due to the in-plane bond stretching of the sp2 carbons. The functionalization of the graphene quantum dots entails introduction of defects onto the GQDs carbon framework. The defects on GQDs are quantified by the ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG). The ID/IG ratio increase from 0.13 for pristine GQDs to 0.18 for NH2GQDs, suggesting higher defects and disorders from decoration of GQDs with the amino groups. However, the ID/IG ratio of rGQDs was the highest (0.21) amongst the three GQDs, showing the highest defects and disorders. This may be because upon reduction, there was elimination of various groups (OH, COOH) and introduction of new groups (CNNH2) which resulted in the disruption of the sp2 carbon framework [42–44]. The incorporation of CoPc onto GQDs results in decoration and structural modification of the GQDs. The following observations were made for:
(i)
covalently formed conjugates: Shifts in the D and G bands of the resulting conjugates were observed. For rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc the D and G bands were observed at 1593 and 1478, and 1591 and 1475, respectively. In addition to the shift, an increase in the ID/IG ratio was observed at 0.58 and 0.44 for rGQDs and NH2GQDs, respectively. The increased ID/IG may be symbolic of further disorders and defect formation upon conjugation.
(ii)
Non-covalently formed conjugates: Non-covalent π-interactions compared to covalent interaction result in minimal disruption of the structural integrity of both interacting compounds. This is further confirmed by the minimal shifts in the D and G bands of the rGQDs(π)CoPc and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc compared to the respective GQDs. There was a moderate increase on the ID/IG values 0.23 and 0.20 for rGQDs(π)CoPc and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc, respectively, compared to the corresponding rGQDs (at 0.21) and NH2GQDs (at 0.18).
The broadening and shifts of the G and D band may be associated with further disruption of the sp2 carbon framework upon conjugation.

To further gain insight of the GQDs chemical structure, FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to confirm functional group existence, Fig. 6 . The peaks of pristine GQDs appearing at frequencies 3348 cm−1, 1540 cm−1, and 1488 cm−1 are attributed to the functional groups COH, CO and CC, respectively. After reduction, there is a decrease in the intensity of the all the peaks that belong to the oxygen containing functional groups. The decrease in intensity is indicative of the elimination of significant oxygen atoms and in turn successful reduction. The amino functionalization of the GQDs to form NH2GQDs was confirmed by the occurrence of two amino peaks around 1134 cm−1 and 3358 cm−1, symbolic of a CN bond formation and primary amine (~NH2) respectively (figure not shown). Similar peaks were also observed for the rGQDs (1130 cm−1 and 3550 cm−1). Upon conjugation of GQDs to the CoPc (rGQDs@CoPc as an example in Fig. 6), similar functional groups were observed with slight shifts in frequency, which confirms structural changes [45]. Most significantly, in both the FT-IR data of the conjugates: rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc an amide stretch (NH) was observed at around 3080 cm−1 and 3060 cm−1 respectively. The existence of the amide stretch serves as a possibility of a successful covalent conjugation to form the rGQDs@CoPc complex. However, such conclusions for the NH2GQDs@CoPc cannot be made as NH2GQDs have an amide stretch on their own, and for this reason, XPS was employed.

The full range XPS spectra of rGQDs and NH2GQDs are shown in Fig. 7A. The XPS spectra show the characteristic C 1s (285 eV) and O 1s (532 eV) peaks for all GQDs. Additional to the dominant carbon and oxygen peaks, nitrogen (N 1s ca. 400.1) peak was observed for rGQDs and NH2GQDs confirming the introduction of nitrogen containing functional groups. The nitrogen content was higher for rGQDs compared to NH2GQDs, Table 2 .

The C:O ratio for the pristine GQDs (1.72) increased to 3.03 and 4.55 for NH2GQDs and rGQDs, respectively, Table 2, thereby confirming elimination of excessive oxygen groups by amino functionalization and reduction of the GQDs. The chemical environment and type of the nitrogen groups in the functionalized GQDs is depicted in the high-resolution spectra of N1s, Fig. 7B, which shows the presence of NH2 groups and other N containing groups.

The high-resolution spectra of C 1s showed in Fig. 7C shows the change in the carbon chemical environment from GQDs to rGQDs and NH2GQDs. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s for pristine GQDs was deconvoluted to four peaks corresponding to CC (sp2), CC (sp3), CO and CO functional groups. CN, CN and OCNH peaks were observed for both NH2GQDs and rGQDs, confirming successful functionalization, since these peaks were not present for pristine GQDs. The reduction of the pristine GQDs by hydrazine is further confirmed by drastic decrease in the peak intensities that correspond to the oxygen containing groups (CO and CO). This further confirms the advantage of hydrazine as being a stronger reducing agent than the well-known NaBH4, since reduction using NaBH4 cannot form perfect sp2 domains but increases OH groups in GQDs planar system, which correspond to minimal reduction of the GQD energy band gap [35].

The functionalization of the GQDs with NH2 groups allows for conjugation to a CoPc with COOH terminal via an amide linkage. The amide bond formation as a result of GQDs/CoPc conjugation is depicted in the high-resolution spectra for rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc, Fig. 7C. Both rGQDs and NH2GQDs have amide bonds (OCNH), but upon conjugation to CoPc the intensity of these bonds increases, Table 2, showing that there more amide bonds. A shift in the binding energies of the different functional groups confirms structural rearrangement.

Before the electrocatalytic detection was hydrazine was performed, cyclic voltammetry was employed to characterize the modified electrodes. Fig. 8a shows a selection of cyclic voltammograms for the modified glassy carbon electrodes in the presence of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe. The cathodic to anodic peak separations (ΔEp) for the modified electrodes are listed in Table 3 .The bare GCE (BGCE) had a ΔEp value closest to the theoretical Nernst value, confirming its good electron transfer kinetics. The observed changes in ΔEp values upon modifications confirm alteration on the BGCE surface. The non-covalent conjugates: GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc had a lower ΔEp when compared to the corresponding covalent conjugates, GCE/rGQDs@CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc, showing the advantage of preserving the π structure of GQDs. A large ΔEp has also been attributed to adsorption of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− [46] on graphene electrodes. Thus, the differences in adsorption behaviour could explain the differences in ΔEp between the covalent and non-covalent conjugates. Important to note; the good electron transfer in ferricyanide is independent of the catalytic activity of the probes towards hydrazine detection.

The redox and structural features of the modified surface were further probed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (IES). Fig. 9 shows the obtained EIS (Nyquist plots) obtained in 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 M KCl for bare GCE (a), GCE/rGQDs (b), GCE/CoPc (c), GCE/rGQDs@CoPc (d) and GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc (e). In a Nyquist plot of an EIS spectrum, the semicircle part at higher frequencies corresponds to an electron-transfer-limited process while the linear part at lower frequencies indicates a diffusion-controlled process. The semicircle diameter is related to the resistance of the electron transfer process [27,47]. There is a very small semicircle domain of the bare glassy carbon electrode, implying a very low electron-transfer resistance of the redox probe. A large semicircle was obtained for GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc, indicating that the diffusion of the electrochemical probe towards the electrode surface might have been hindered. This might be due to possible aggregation of adjacent Pcs during π-π stacking on the electrode surface. Very small (or none) circles were obtained for GCE/GQDs, GCE/CoPc and GCE/rGQDs@CoPc indicative of a simple diffusion-controlled electrochemical process.

Fig. 10 shows the comparative cyclic voltammograms of the modified electrodes in response to the detection of hydrazine, all at a scan rate of 100 mV/s. Double oxidation peaks were observed especially clear in some cases in Fig. 10. The electro-oxidation on both scans has been attributed to the regeneration or reformation of the active catalyst that oxidises hydrazine [48].The obtained oxidation potentials for all the used probes are listed in Table 3.

The non-covalent conjugates (GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc) show less favourable potentials (more positive) for hydrazine oxidation when compared to the corresponding covalent conjugates, GCE/rGQDs@CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc. However in terms of currents, the former performed better. The non-covalent interactions between GQDs and CoPc enhance electron transfer due to extended π-electron system hence improved catalytic behaviour (in terms of current) for GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc and GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc compared to covalently linked CoPc. The highest catalytic currents were obtained for GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc. GCE/CoPc showed good catalytic potentials and relatively high currents. Good catalytic activity is judged by both the lowering of potentials and increase in currents. CoPc alone showed low potential but the oxidation currents were not as favourable as for GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc, hence the presence of GQDs enhances the sensitivity as will be shown below when discussing limits of detection. The good performance of the CoPc alone or in GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc may be because of the push-pull effects of the substituents on the Pc, which have been reported to enhance electron delocalization thus, electron transfer properties of the species [49]. The push-pull effects of its substituents are preserved when not covalently bound to GQDs. rGQDs containing electrodes performed better than the NH2GQDs in terms of current when compared to the linked conjugates. The improved electron transfer properties may be attributed to the elimination of excessive oxygen atoms on the GQDs (to yield rGQDs) surface upon reduction which are directly proportional to the energy band gap [5,50,51] of the GQDs and hence electron transfer abilities.

The following reaction mechanism has been proposed for electrocatalytic oxidation of hydrazine on CoPc derivatives [52] Eqs. (1) to (4)
(1)
Co
I
Pc
surface −
↔
Co
II
Pc
surface
+
e −
(2)
Co
II
Pc
surface
+
N 2
H 4
+
OH −
→ rds
Co
I
Pc …
N 2
H 3
surface −
+
H 2
O
(3)
Co
I
Pc …
N 2
H 3
surface −
→
Co
I
Pc
surface −
+ ∗
N 2
H 3
(4)
∗
N 2
H 3
+ 3
OH −
→ fast
N 2
+ 3
e −
+ 3
H 2
O

This mechanism shows the involvement of the central Co metal in electrocatalysis. During the electrooxidation process the hydrazine molecule interacts with the metal centre, causing electron drain from one of the lone pair electrons of the nitrogen atoms of hydrazine towards the central metal forming a bond in Co(I)Pc-N2H3. Thus the electron transfer occurs by charge transfer through a bond and not through space. The active species Co(II)Pc is reduced to Co(I) by hydrazine undergoing oxidation, Eq. (2). The Co(I)Pc-N2H3 is decomposed, Eq. (3), with N2H3 released into solution to react with −OH ions from the basic media to form N2, Eq. (4). From Co(I)Pc, Co(II)Pc can be generated, allowing repetition of the process.

Fig. 11 shows 20 continuous scan cyclic voltammograms for GCE/CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc as examples in 1 mM hydrazine. For the NH2GQDs@CoPc modified GCE, a percentage loss in current of 5% from cycle 1 to 2 and 8% after 20 cycles was observed, followed by stabilization thereafter. All electrodes were tested for stability on going from first to second cycle in the cyclic voltammetric scans and the order in terms of stability was GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc (2%) > GCE/CoPc (3%) > GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc (5%) > GCE/rGQDs (6%) > GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc (8%) > GCE/NH2GQDs [9] and GCE/rGQDs@CoPc (10%) for hydrazine, Table 3. Thus, GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc is more stable than GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc, but the latter performs better in terms of current.

To determine the reversibility of the catalysed oxidation reaction, multiple cycles at various scan rates were run, Fig. 12 shows GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc as an example. A shift in the peak with increase in scan rate confirms irreversibility of the process (Fig. 12a). Fig. 12c shows the plot of current versus the square root of sweep rate for hydrazine. A linear correlation is observed, confirming that the oxidation process is diffusion controlled. The relationship between peak potential and scan rate for an irreversible and diffusion controlled is given by Eq. (5) [53].
(5)
E p
=
b 2
log
v + K
where b is the Tafel slope and v is the scan rate.

Fig. 12b shows a plot of Ep vs log v; a linear relationship was obtained. Tafel slopes within the 60–120 mV·decade−1 for a one electron rate determining step were obtained in some cases, Table 3. The higher than the normal 60–120 mV·decade−1 may suggest adsorption of either the products and/or intermediates on the modified electrode surface [54].

To further probe the kinetics of the electrooxidation of hydrazine on a glassy carbon electrode, the electrochemical reactions were carried out using rotating disc electrode (RDE), Fig. 13 . The GCE/CoPc and GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc were analysed as they had comparable activity and performed better than the rest of the electrodes. The typical voltammograms of hydrazine oxidation obtained on the GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc rotating disk electrode as a function of the electrode rotation rates are shown in Fig. 13A. A trend where the current increases with increase in the rotating rate of the RDE was observed. Linear correlations indicating diffusion controlled mass transport were obtained from plots of limiting current (IL) vs ω1/2 (ω is the rotating rate of the RDE), Fig. 13A (insert) and C (C is concentration of hydrazine), Fig. 13B (insert). To further demonstrate the superiority of the GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc compared to GCE/CoPc, polarization curves of both modified electrodes are plotted, Fig. 13C. Tafel slopes of both electrodes were calculated in from the polarization curves in the kinetic region. Lower Tafel slopes were obtained for the GCE/CoPc (110 mV/decade) compared to GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc (145 mV/decade). The obtained results for GCE/CoPc may suggest that the transfer coefficient of a single electron is the rate determining step (assuming that α = 0.41), and for GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc (0.59) that the rate determining step is preceded by an electron transfer [27,55].

The chronoamperometric data obtained was employed to determine the catalytic rate constant for the oxidation of hydrazine. Catalytic rate constants are a measure of the rate at which the oxidation process occurs at the electrode/analyte interface. Fig. 14a shows chronoamperometric evolutions at various concentrations for rGQDs(π)CoPc (as an example) all in 1 M, NaOH. Plots of Icat/Ibuff vs. square root of time (Fig. 13b and Eq. (6)) within rapid decay region (<2 s) were linear with varying slopes.
(6)
Icat Ibuf
=
γ
1
2
π
1
2
=
π
1 2
kCt
1 2
where I
cat and I
buff represent the currents in the presence and absence of hydrazine respectively, k the catalytic rate constant, C the bulk concentration hydrazine and t the time elapsed in seconds.

Plotting the square of the slopes (from Fig. 14b) against the respective concentrations gave linear plots (figure not shown) whose slope is equal to πk where k is the rate constant. The plots are represented by Eqs. (7i) to (7vii).
(i)
GCE/CoPc
(7i)
y = 204.13
N 2
H 4
,
R 2
= 0.9402
(ii)
GCE/rGQDs
(7ii)
y = 7.4424
N 2

R 2
= 0.9878
(iii)
GCE/rGQDs@CoPc
(7iii)
y = 8.8581

= 0.9556
(iv)
GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc
(7iv)
y = 249.76

= 0.9394
(v)
GCE/NH2GQDs
(7v)
y = 27.911 x
N 2
H 4

= 0.9309
(vi)
GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc
(7vi)
y = 5.9648
N 2

= 0.9943
(vii)
GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc
(7vii)
y = 10.1625
N 2
H 4
,
R 2
= 0.9856

The largest k value of 7.9 × 104 M−1 s−1 was observed for the GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc probe, Table 3. This value was larger than for the covalent GCE/rGQDs@CoPc conjugate, Table 3. However, GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc gave a larger k value than the corresponding GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc). The rGQDs containing conjugates gave better k values than the corresponding NH2GQDs conjugates, Table 3, showing the importance of reduction of GQDs.

Using the plots of concentration vs. current obtained from chronoamperometric data (Fig. 14a, insert) limits of detection (LODs) of all the probes were determined, Table 4 . The lowest detection limits were obtained for probes GCE/CoPc and GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc. The non-covalent conjugates (GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs(π)CoPc) more favourable LOD for hydrazine oxidation when compared to the corresponding covalent conjugates, GCE/rGQDs@CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc. The rGQDs containing conjugates gave better LOD values than the corresponding NH2GQDs conjugates, Table 4, which may suggest superior sensitivity of the rGQDs over NH2GQDs towards hydrazine. The LOD reported for rGQDs(π)CoPc (LOD = 2.1 μM) and CoPc (3.2 μM) in this work are lower than the reported values for other CoPc towards electrooxidation of hydrazine, even when in the presence of pristine GQDs, due to the push pull effect of complex 3, Table 4.

The rGQDs containing electrodes displayed higher electrocatalytic behaviour towards detection of hydrazine when compared to the NH2GQDs containing ones. The conductivity of GQDs in general is highly dependent on its structure as per determined by the energy band gap. The rGQDs were manufactured such that there was elimination of the excessive oxygen moieties on the surface, and introduction with new nitrogen contain groups on the edges. The NH2GQDs on the other hand, are formed solely from peripheral modification while still maintaining the oxygenated core structure. As a result, there was intense narrowing of the energy band gap of the rGQDs compared to NH2GQDs, hence better electrocatalytic performance.

The use of tris-tert-butyl substituted push-pull cobalt phthalocyanine permits directionality of charge transfer from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level to the electrode surface, which then results in enhanced electrocatalytic activity [56]. The covalent conjugation of the CoPc (3) to graphene quantum dots resulted in the disruption of its push pull effect (Scheme 3A). As a result, GCE/CoPc has better electrocatalytic behaviour than GCE/rGQDs@CoPc and GCE/NH2GQDs@CoPc.

The non-covalent interactions of the CoPc (3) to the rGQDs and NH2GQDs result in the extension of its π-electron system. Extension therefore of the π system aids increased electron transfer abilities, which also enhances the electrocatalytic activity of the CoPc/GQDs complex, compared to CoPc alone. The difference in activity of the rGQDs(π)CoPc and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc is attributed the difference in chemical make-up of the planar GQD system as governed by the modification employed.

Cobalt tris-(tert-butylphenoxy)-monocarboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (CoPc, 3) was conjugated (covalently) to rGQDs and NH2GQDs to give rGQDs@CoPc and NH2GQDs@CoPc respectively and through π-π interactions to give GQDs(π)CoPc and NH2GQDs(π)CoPc, respectively. The resulting conjugates were used for the electrocatalysis of hydrazine on a glassy carbon electrode. Low LOD values were obtained for GCE/CoPc, and GCE/rGQDs(π)CoPc. The obtained results confirmed that elimination of excessive oxygen containing groups on the surface of GQDs does indeed tune the energy band gap and results in enhanced electron transfer and conductivity. Where phthalocyanines are concerned, the results confirm the enhanced electron transfer properties via the push pull effects because of the substituents contained in the phthalocyanine, which played a significant role in its electrocatalytic behaviour. The improved catalytic behaviour upon π-stacking compared to covalent linkage of CoPc to the functionalized graphene quantum dots is symbolic of the significance in the preservation of the π-structure of GQDs and the CoPc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), and Rhodes University. SSC would like to thank NRF for Masters funding.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.05.005.
